label: moerman::echinacea_14nations
text: Echinacea purpurea / E. angustifolia — Purple Coneflower. Moerman's Native American Ethnobotany Database. Documented across 14 independent nations: Lakota (root for toothache, cold, snakebite), Cheyenne (cough), Comanche (wound application), Kiowa (sore throat), Pawnee (skin disease), Omaha (infected wounds, gonorrhoea), Delaware (venereal disease), Fox (snakebite). Internal convergence across 14 nations with no documented inter-group transmission on this plant. Preparation: root chewed raw, decocted, juice applied. Active: alkamides, polysaccharides, caffeic acid derivatives. Cochrane systematic review (2015): modest but real reduction in cold duration and severity. Immune cell activation documented in vitro and in vivo.

label: moerman::salix_fever_multiplenations
text: Salix species — Willow for Pain and Fever. Moerman's database. Cherokee (S. nigra): bark decocted for fever and pain. Ojibwe: bark for headache and pain. Iroquois: bark for fever. Menominee: bark for pain. Potawatomi: fever. Micmac: fever. Geographic distribution: Cherokee (Southeast), Ojibwe (Great Lakes), Iroquois (Northeast), Menominee (Midwest) — no documented pre-contact inter-group transmission. Combined with Egyptian (1550 BCE), Greek (400 BCE), Chinese, and Islamic convergent documentation, willow bark for pain and fever represents the most globally validated convergence signal in this archive. Densmore (1928): Chippewa preferred inner bark in spring — pharmacologically rational as salicylate content peaks in spring growth. Active: salicin → salicylic acid (aspirin precursor).

label: moerman::goldenseal_berberine
text: Hydrastis canadensis — Goldenseal. Moerman database. Cherokee: root tea for digestive complaints, liver, eye wash. Iroquois: whooping cough, heart trouble, local infections. Ojibwe: eye infections. Creek: general tonic. Lewis and Clark (1804): recorded use and introduced to European awareness. Active: berberine, canadine, hydrastine. Berberine: documented antimicrobial (MRSA-active), antifungal, antiparasitic. Multiple clinical trials: berberine comparable to metformin for Type 2 diabetes blood glucose management. Convergence: berberine in Coptis (Kampo/Chinese), Berberis (Ayurvedic), Hydrastis (North American) — three independent traditions discovered berberine-containing plants for infection.

label: moerman::yarrow_46nations
text: Achillea millefolium — Yarrow. Moerman database: documented by 46 different nations — the most widely used medicinal plant among North American indigenous peoples in the database. Cherokee: fever; Cheyenne: headache; Blackfoot: internal bleeding; Ojibwe: wound dressing; Lakota: head colds; Navajo: wound poultice; Zuni: wound hemostasis. European folk medicine: identical applications. Greek/Roman: Achilles reportedly used on battlefield wounds (genus named Achillea). Cross-tradition convergence for wound healing and fever across Native American, European folk, and Greek/Roman traditions. Active: chamazulene, salicylic acid derivatives, flavonoids. Hemostatic mechanism: promotes platelet aggregation and vasoconstriction.

label: moerman::cascara_pharmaceutical
text: Rhamnus purshiana — Cascara Sagrada. Pacific Northwest nations: Quinault, Squaxin, Cowlitz, Colville, Chetco, Klallam (bark tea as laxative). Spanish missionaries named it 'sacred bark'. J.H. Bundy (1877) introduced to US medicine from indigenous documentation. 1890-1970s: one of the most widely prescribed laxatives in North America. Active: cascarosides A, B, C, D (hydroxyanthracene glycosides) — stimulate colonic motility. FDA (2002): moved to prescription-only status due to inadequate long-term safety data. Represents one of the cleanest indigenous-to-pharmaceutical transfers in North American medicine — documented traditional use led directly to pharmaceutical development with identified active mechanism.

label: moerman::lobelia_asthma
text: Lobelia inflata — Indian Tobacco / Pukeweed. Cherokee: root tea for asthma and whooping cough. Iroquois: root for respiratory complaints. Penobscot: smoked for asthma. Densmore (1928): Chippewa use of Lobelia smoke for chest conditions. Active: lobeline (piperidine alkaloid) — acts on nicotinic acetylcholine receptors, producing bronchodilation and expectorant effects at therapeutic doses, emesis at higher doses. Used as smoking cessation aid in early 20th century before nicotine replacement therapy. Modern research: lobeline and analogs actively studied for ADHD and drug dependence (methamphetamine antagonism via dopaminergic reward pathway). Three independent nations identified same plant for asthma — strong internal convergence.

label: moerman::usnea_antimicrobial
text: Usnea species — Old Man's Beard Lichen. Moerman database. Cowlitz, Okanagan-Colville, Nlaka'pamux, Blackfoot: Usnea applied to infected wounds. Consistent documentation across geographically separated Pacific Northwest nations for the same application. Active: usnic acid (dibenzofuran) — broad-spectrum antimicrobial against gram-positive bacteria including Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Currently under investigation for drug-resistant tuberculosis. Also antiprotozoal (Leishmania), anti-inflammatory, antiviral. Indigenous use pharmacologically precise — usnic acid targets gram-positive bacteria, the most common wound pathogens.

label: moerman::american_ginseng_convergence
text: Panax quinquefolius — American Ginseng. Cherokee: tonic, aphrodisiac, headache. Iroquois: fever, digestive, female disorders. Ojibwe: tonic for weak and elderly. Meskwaki: menstrual cramps. Active: ginsenosides (different profile from Asian P. ginseng — higher Rb1 vs Rg1 in Asian). Multiple trials: blood sugar regulation (Type 2 diabetes — positive); cognitive function (modest positive); immune modulation. Convergence: Panax ginseng documented in Chinese and Kampo traditions; Panax quinquefolius documented independently by multiple North American nations — same genus independently identified on opposite sides of the Pacific with similar medicinal properties. One of the clearest examples of convergent pharmacological discovery across civilisations.

label: moerman::sweat_lodge_thermal
text: Sweat Lodge Medicine — documented across Plains, Southwest, Northwest Coast, Eastern Woodlands, Subarctic traditions. Lakota: inipi. Medical applications: fever management; respiratory conditions (steam with medicinal plants); skin diseases; joint pain; mental health clearing. Plants added to steam (Densmore 1928): cedar (antimicrobial), sweetgrass (anxiolytic aroma), sage/Artemisia (antimicrobial). Modern research: hyperthermia activates heat-shock proteins with immune-modulating effects; sauna therapy validated for cardiovascular health; steam inhalation with plant aromatics documented for respiratory benefit. The sweat lodge as a systematic thermal medical institution documented independently across multiple North American cultural groups represents the most sophisticated pre-Columbian thermal medicine technology in North America.

label: moerman::tobacco_nicotiana_medicine
text: Nicotiana rustica / N. tabacum — Tobacco (medicinal applications distinct from ritual). Iroquois, Cherokee: leaves as wound poultice. Crow: smoke blown into painful ear. Blackfoot: leaves for insect bites and bee stings. Zuni: topical anesthetic before minor procedures (documented by Stevenson, 1909). Pharmacology of medicinal use: nicotine as insecticide (insect bites); topical nicotine blocks pain signaling at acetylcholine receptors (surgical anesthetic mechanism); cotinine (metabolite) anti-inflammatory. The Zuni topical anesthetic application represents a sophisticated empirical pharmacological discovery — nicotine blocks nAChR at neuromuscular junctions, producing localised insensitivity. Indigenous medicine carefully distinguished between medicinal and ritual tobacco use.
